attack_chain.description: Attacker exploited the Shellshock (CVE-2014-6271) vulnerability against an old, unpatched prototype EC2 machine that had been running since before 2012 and was not in active use.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: It was penetrated using the shellshock vulnerability, and since it was no longer in active use, it did not have the appropriate patch installed.

attack_chain.technique_name: Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files (AWS API Keys)
attack_chain.description: After gaining shell access on the prototype machine, the attacker found the AWS API access key and secret key stored on the machine.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The old prototype machine had our AWS API access key and secret key. Once the hacker gained access to the keys...

attack_chain.description: Using the stolen AWS API credentials, the attacker created a new IAM user and generated a key-pair to establish persistent, independent access to the AWS account.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Once the hacker gained access to the keys, he created an IAM user, and generated a key-pair.

attack_chain.technique_name: Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure: Create Cloud Instance
attack_chain.description: With the newly created IAM user credentials, the attacker launched a new EC2 instance within BrowserStack's AWS account to use as an operational platform.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: He was then able to run an instance inside our AWS account using these credentials, and mount one of our backup disks.

attack_chain.technique_name: Data from Cloud Storage (Backup EBS Volume)
attack_chain.description: The attacker mounted one of BrowserStack's backup EBS disks on their rogue EC2 instance and accessed its contents, discovering a config file containing the production database password.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: mount one of our backup disks. This backup was of one of our component services, used for production environment, and contained a config file with our database password.

attack_chain.technique_name: Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files (DB Password in Config)
attack_chain.description: Attacker retrieved the production database password from a plaintext config file found on the mounted backup disk.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: contained a config file with our database password

attack_chain.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Firewall
attack_chain.description: The attacker modified the AWS database security group to whitelist their own IP address, granting direct external access to the production database.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: He also whitelisted his IP on our database security group, which is the AWS firewall.

attack_chain.technique_name: Data from Information Repositories (Database Table Copy)
attack_chain.description: Using the stolen database password and newly whitelisted IP, the attacker connected to the production database and began copying a table containing user email IDs, hashed passwords, and last tested URLs.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: He began to copy one of our tables, which contained partial user information, including email IDs, hashed passwords, and last tested URL.

attack_chain.description: The attacker exfiltrated a partial copy of the user data table before being detected. The table lock triggered monitoring alerts and BrowserStack blocked the unrecognized IP.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: His copy operation locked the database table, which raised alerts on our monitoring system. On receiving the alerts, we checked the logs, saw an unrecognized IP, and blocked it right away. In that time, the hacker had been able to retrieve only a portion of the data.

attack_chain.technique_name: Application Layer Protocol: Mail Protocols (SES Abuse)
attack_chain.description: Using the exfiltrated email addresses and SES credentials found during the intrusion, the attacker sent a fraudulent email to approximately 5,000 BrowserStack users claiming the service was shutting down.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: using this data and the SES credentials, he was able to send an email to some of our users.

initial_access_vector: Shellshock (CVE-2014-6271) exploitation against an unpatched, inactive prototype EC2 machine exposed to the internet
persistence_mechanism: Creation of a new IAM user with a generated key-pair using stolen AWS API access keys from the compromised prototype machine
privilege_escalation: Used AWS API access key and secret key stored on the compromised prototype machine to call IAM APIs, creating a new IAM user with an independent key-pair — effectively escalating from EC2 instance-level access to full AWS account-level access
